hints: Digits are independent: d₉∈{8,9}, d₀∈{0,1}, dᵢ∈{i−1,i,i+1} for i=1..8.
Average = Σ E[dᵢ]·10ⁱ with E[dᵢ]=i (i=1..8), E[d₉]=8.5, E[d₀]=0.5.
solution: The digits are chosen independently with $\mathbb{E}[d_i]=i$ for $i=1..8$, $\mathbb{E}[d_9]=8.5$, $\mathbb{E}[d_0]=0.5$. So the average is $8.5\cdot10^9+\sum_{i=1}^{8}i\cdot10^i+0.5=8.5\cdot10^9+876543210+0.5=\dfrac{18753086421}{2}.$

hints: Track Julie's net (start 0); break even = back to 0 in 8 steps without hitting ±4 (bankruptcy).
Paths 0→0 in 8 steps: C(8,4)=70; subtract those touching +4 or −4 (1 each by reflection).
solution: Let the net change start at $0$; breaking even means returning to $0$ in $8$ steps without reaching $\pm4$ (a bankruptcy). There are $\binom84=70$ return paths; by reflection $1$ touches $+4$ and $1$ touches $-4$ (none both). So $70-2=68$ valid paths, giving $P=\dfrac{68}{256}=\dfrac{17}{64}.$

hints: The contract is a call struck at 40; only acquisition outcomes can exceed 40.
solution: The contract pays $\max(\text{price}-40,0)$. Only acquisition matters: $\mathbb{E}=0.2\big[0.5(10)+0.5\max(x-40,0)\big]=1+0.1\max(x-40,0)$. Setting this to $15$ gives $\max(x-40,0)=140$, so $x=180.$

hints: Work backwards from 409, inverting each raid (took a fraction plus a fractional sheep).
solution: Working backwards: after the third raid $\tfrac{4c}5-\tfrac35=409\Rightarrow c=512$; before the second $\tfrac{3b}4-\tfrac14=512\Rightarrow b=683$; before the first $\tfrac{2a}3-\tfrac13=683\Rightarrow a=1025.$

hints: Groups are the cycles of a random permutation; average group size = n / E[#cycles].
solution: The groups are cycles of a random permutation, so the average group size is $\dfrac{n}{\mathbb{E}[\#\text{cycles}]}=\dfrac{50}{H_{50}}\approx\dfrac{50}{4.499}\approx11.1.$

hints: P(red from urn k)=1/2^k; posterior on urn k after a red ∝ (1/2^k).
solution: Urn $k$ gives red with probability $\tfrac1{2^k}$. After a red, the posterior on urn $k$ is $\propto\tfrac1{2^k}$, so $P(\text{2nd red})=\dfrac{\sum_k(1/2^k)^2}{\sum_k 1/2^k}$. As $n\to\infty$ this is $\dfrac{\sum1/4^k}{\sum1/2^k}=\dfrac{1/3}{1}=\dfrac13.$

hints: Variance is maximized by pushing all mass to the extremes of [−1,1].
Y=±1 each with probability 1/2 has mean 0.
solution: Variance on $[-1,1]$ is largest when the mass sits entirely at the endpoints: $Y=\pm1$ each w.p. $\tfrac12$ gives mean $0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=1$, which is the maximum.

hints: Every group of 3 must be missing some lock; assign one lock per 3-subset, held by the other 4.
n=C(7,3)=35 locks; each person holds locks for 3-subsets not containing them: m=C(6,3)=20.
solution: For each of the $\binom73=35$ triples, create a lock that none of those three can open (the other four hold its key). Then any $4$ people together cover all locks, but no $3$ do, so $n=35$. Each person holds keys for triples excluding them: $m=\binom63=20$. Thus $10m+n=200+35=235.$

hints: The number of winning lines in n×n×n is ((n+2)³−n³)/2.
solution: Embedding the $n^3$ board in an $(n+2)^3$ cube, the number of lines is $\dfrac{(n+2)^3-n^3}{2}$. For $n=4$: $\dfrac{216-64}{2}=76.$

hints: For the person knowing i others, P(knows ≥1 neighbour)=1 − (N−1−i)(N−2−i)/((N−1)(N−2)), N=2m.
Average over i=0..N−1; the sum of (N−1−i)(N−2−i) telescopes via Σj(j−1)=N(N−1)(N−2)/3.
solution: Let $N=2m$. The person knowing $i$ others fails to know either neighbour with probability $\dfrac{(N-1-i)(N-2-i)}{(N-1)(N-2)}$. Averaging over $i$, $\dfrac1N\sum_i\dfrac{(N-1-i)(N-2-i)}{(N-1)(N-2)}=\dfrac1N\cdot\dfrac{N(N-1)(N-2)/3}{(N-1)(N-2)}=\dfrac13$. So the expected proportion knowing a neighbour is $1-\tfrac13=\tfrac23.$

hints: Choose exactly 2 of {1,2,3}, then anything from the rest.
solution: Pick $2$ of $\{1,2,3\}$ ($\binom32=3$ ways) and any subset of the remaining $7$ elements ($2^7=128$): $3\cdot128=384.$
